summary:
Michelangelo di Lodovico Buonarroti Simoni (1475–1564 AD) was an Italian sculptor, painter, architect, and poet of the [[event:2238|Italian Renaissance]] who is widely considered the greatest artist of all time, a figure whose works — the marble statue of David (1501–1504), the Pietà (1498–1499), the Sistine Chapel ceiling frescoes (1508–1512), and the dome of St. Peter's Basilica — defined the High Renaissance and established standards of artistic achievement that shaped Western art for centuries. Born near Florence, Michelangelo trained under the sculptor Bertoldo di Giovanni and came under the patronage of Lorenzo de' Medici, entering the extraordinary cultural world of Laurentian Florence where he absorbed Neoplatonist philosophy, classical sculpture, and humanist learning that would inform his lifelong artistic vision.

Michelangelo's conception of sculpture as 'releasing the figure from the stone' — finding the form already present within the marble — produced works of unparalleled technical mastery and expressive power. The David, carved from a massive block of Carrara marble that other sculptors had rejected, combined classical idealism with unprecedented psychological intensity. The Sistine Chapel ceiling, painted lying on scaffolding over four years, depicted the entire Genesis narrative from Creation to the Flood with a grandeur and anatomical mastery that transformed fresco painting. His later work — including The Last Judgment (Sistine Chapel, 1536–1541) and his architectural designs for St. Peter's — moved toward the restless, mannerist tension that influenced later Baroque art. Michelangelo lived to 88, working until his final days, and was the first Western artist to have a biography written during his lifetime — a measure of his contemporaries' recognition that they were witnessing something unprecedented.
